paragraphs.text: Having spoken of the nakedness of the first parents, which under their heavenly clothing was becoming and gave no occasion for shame, Moses turns to recount the cunning of the serpent and says: “Now the serpent was more subtle than any beast of the field which the Lord God had made” (Gen 3:1). The serpent was more cunning than the unreasoning animals over whom man ruled; but although it exceeded in cunning the level appointed to beasts, it does not follow that it rose to the level of man. God created the serpent more cunning than the unreasoning cattle and craftier than the irrational animals; but since it had no reason, it is clear that it did not have human wisdom either. Adam surpassed the serpent by the very mode of his creation, by his soul, by his understanding, by the glory in which he was clothed, and by his dwelling place; and so it is plain that he was immeasurably higher than the serpent in cunning too. Adam surpassed in wisdom all the beasts, because God set him as their lord and ruler; he was craftier than all, because he gave all of them names. As the Israelites could not without a veil look upon the face of Moses, so the animals could not look upon Adam’s bright countenance. With downcast eyes they passed before him when he named them, because their eyes could not bear his glory. Therefore, although the serpent was more cunning than the beasts, it was unreasoning before Adam and Eve, the lords of the beasts.

Having spoken of the serpent’s cunning, Moses turns to recount its evil-contriving approach to Eve, and says: “And the serpent said unto the woman: Hath God indeed said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?” (Gen 3:1). The serpent spoke; and this was either a hiss proper to the serpent, which Adam understood, or Satan spoke in the serpent, or the serpent by its own contrivance begged for itself the gift of speech, or Satan asked God to give the gift of speech to the serpent for a time. But the tempting word would not have led the tempted into sin had not their own desire served the tempter as a guide. Even had the tempter not come, the tree by its beauty would have drawn them into a struggle. Although the first parents sought an excuse for themselves in the counsel of the serpent, yet more than the counsel of the serpent it was their own desire that injured them. For it is said: “And the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes… and a tree to be desired: and she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat” (Gen 3:6). If the woman was overcome by the beauty of the tree and the sweetness of its fruits, she was not overcome by the counsel that had entered her ears. She was led into the transgression by a desire that appeared in herself. But since the commandment had been given for testing, a fitting occasion came for the tempter.

All that was created in paradise and outside paradise God gave to man out of goodness and required nothing from him for what He had created, but adorned him and clothed him with glory. Everything that was in paradise, on the earth, in the air, and in the waters, all of it was given to man out of goodness; but one tree alone was taken from him by righteousness.

When God created man, He did not make him mortal, but neither did He make him immortal, so that Adam himself, by keeping or transgressing the commandment, might from one of the two trees acquire what he wished. And although God had created the tree of life, He hid it from Adam; first, that its beauty might not draw the first parents into a struggle and increase their conflict; and second, that they should not, in order to keep the commandment of the Unseen One, find their motive (seek their encouragement) in a reward that was before their eyes. Although by goodness God gave the first parents everything, yet the immortal life that was to be acquired by eating the fruit of the tree of life He willed to grant them by righteousness. Therefore He gave them only a commandment, and this commandment was not so great; it could not equal the surpassing reward prepared for them. But by forbidding them to eat the fruit of one tree, that they might be under a commandment, God gave them the whole of paradise, so that they should not be compelled into transgression by lack of food.

But although, as I have said, the testing was needful, yet God did not allow Satan to send any angel, seraph, or cherub to Adam for that purpose. Nor did He allow Satan himself to come to Adam in the garden of Eden in a human or divine form, as he approached our Lord on the mountain. Nor did any great or better beasts come to Adam — neither Behemoth nor Leviathan, nor any other beasts or clean animals — that this might not serve in any way as an excuse for those who transgressed the commandment. The serpent, however, was permitted to come to them, who, though cunning, was beyond measure contemptible and loathsome. The serpent, approaching the people, did no real wonder, did not even take upon itself a false form, but appeared in the form it had: it came creeping, with eyes cast down, because it could not bear the radiance of the face of her whom it sought to tempt. From fear it did not come to Adam, but came to Eve, that it might more easily incline her to eat of the fruit of that tree from which they were forbidden to eat — while she had not yet tasted of the fruits permitted her from thousands upon thousands (ten thousands) of other trees. She had not eaten not because she was fasting, but because hunger had not yet come upon her, for she had only just been created. Of course, the serpent was not forbidden to come so hastily to Eve, for this very haste of the serpent did not work in its favor. It came at a time when Eve, hardly yet created, did not yet know what hunger is, and the beauty of the tree did not stir in her the struggle of desire. So, since Eve did not feel hunger and the tree did not draw her into struggle, the serpent was not forbidden to be her tempter. For if Eve had conquered in this brief war and short struggle, the serpent and the one who was in the serpent would have suffered that very punishment they came to bear; Eve and her husband would have eaten of the fruits of life and acquired eternal life, receiving by righteousness the being that had been promised them, and they would have come to possess by righteousness all that had previously been given them by goodness.

Therefore the tempter hastened to come, and he was not held back. The very fact that the tempter came together with the commandment ought to have made plain to the tempted that he was a tempter, and warned them against his snares. The tempter comes and promises them something great — for indeed he could not boast of anything small. And the one who was in the serpent spoke through it thus to the woman: “Hath God indeed said: Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?” (Gen 3:1). Here it must be noted that the commandment would have been great if it had been forbidden to eat the fruit of every tree, as the serpent says; but since the contrary was commanded, the commandment was almost not even considered a commandment, for it was very light and given only for a time, until the tempter should withdraw from them.

Eve answered the serpent and said: “We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden: but of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die” (Gen 3:2–3). The serpent and the one who was in the serpent, hearing that the fruit of every tree of paradise had been given them for food, and that they were forbidden only the fruit of one tree, were already thinking that they must withdraw in shame, for they saw there was nothing left to promise them. Therefore the tempter directs his attention to the very commandment of the One who gave it, which forbade not only eating the fruit of the tree but even coming near it, and he understood that God was warning them not to look upon the tree lest they be captivated by its beauty; and so he persuades Eve to turn her gaze upon it and says: “Ye shall not surely die. For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil” (Gen 3:4–5). Eve did not weigh the words of the serpent, did not consider that he as a tempter was saying the opposite of what God had said, did not contradict the serpent with his own words and did not say, “How shall my eyes be opened, when they are not closed? And how shall I, having tasted the fruit of the tree, know good and evil, when even before tasting I have such knowledge?” She lost sight of what she should have answered the serpent, and by his wish turned away her eyes from the serpent which was before her, fixing her gaze on the tree to which it was forbidden to draw near.

The serpent was silent, because he had long noticed her guilt. It was not so much the promise heard by Eve that persuaded her to taste of the fruit of that tree as the look she fixed upon the tree that lured her to pluck its fruit and eat. Eve might have said to the serpent: “If I am deprived of sight, how do I see all that is visible? If I cannot distinguish good and evil, how do I judge whether your promise is good or evil? How do I know that to be a god is good, that to have eyes open is desirable? Whence is it known to me that death is evil, if I am deprived of this — and why have you come to me? Your coming to us testifies that we have all this, for your promise I test by the very sight I already have, and by the faculty of distinguishing good and bad which I already possess. But if I have what you promise me, where is all your cunning, when your craftiness has not escaped me?” But Eve did not say this to the serpent, for had she said it, she would have conquered him; she fixed her gaze on the tree, so as the more quickly to be conquered.

Eve, giving herself up to the desire of her eyes and longing to be a god, as the serpent had promised, plucks the forbidden fruit and eats secretly from her husband; and then gives it also to her husband, who eats too. Since Eve had believed the serpent, she hurried and ate before her husband, hoping that, already clothed with divinity, she would return to the one from whom she had come forth as a human being. She hurried to taste before her husband so as to become the head of the one who was her head, to become the commander of the one from whom she ought to have received commands, to appear in divinity older than the one before whom she was younger in humanity. But when she had eaten and was not greater than before, though she had not become less; and had not gained having her eyes opened, because she had not become a god as she had expected, and did not notice that her eyes were opened to see her own nakedness — then she brought the fruit also to her husband, and with many entreaties prevailed on him to eat, although it is not written that she begged him.

So Eve, having eaten, did not die the death, as God had said; but she also did not become a god, as the serpent had said. If her nakedness had been laid bare, Adam would have come to fear and would not have eaten; and though he would not have been guilty as one who did not eat, neither would he have been the victor — as one not subjected to temptation. In that case Adam would have abstained from eating because of his wife’s nakedness, not out of love for the Giver of the commandment or fear of God. But since it was needful that Adam too should not for long be tempted by the seduction of Eve, as she was tempted by the promise of the serpent, Eve drew near to the tree, tasted of its fruit, and her nakedness was not revealed. But when she seduced Adam and he too ate, then, Scripture says, “the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked” (Gen 3:7). So their eyes were opened, but not that they might become gods, as the serpent said, but that they might see their nakedness, which is what the enemy was contriving.

It was for this that the eyes of the first parents had previously been opened, to see all things; and were closed, that they might not see the tree of life or their own nakedness. The enemy envied the first parents, for by glory and by the gift of speech they were higher than all that is upon the earth, and to them alone was promised the eternal life that the tree of life could give. Envying both what Adam had and what he was to acquire, the enemy laid his snares, and in a brief war took away from them what they should not have lost in a long struggle. If the serpent had not drawn them into transgression, they would have eaten of the fruit of the tree of life, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil would then not have been forbidden them, for from one of these trees they would have acquired infallible knowledge, and from the other received eternal life and in their humanity would have become Godlike.

The first parents would have acquired infallible knowledge and immortal life while still in the flesh; but the serpent by his promise robbed them of what they could have acquired, persuading them that they would acquire it by transgression of the commandment — and all this for the one reason that they should not acquire what God had promised through keeping the commandment. Promising that they would be “as gods” (Gen 3:5), he robbed them of this; and so that the promised tree of life should not enlighten their eyes, he promised that the tree of knowledge would open their eyes.

If the first parents had wished, even after the transgression of the commandment, to repent, then though they would not have regained what they had possessed before the transgression of the commandment, at least they would have been delivered from the curses pronounced upon the earth and upon themselves. For God, too, delayed in coming to them so that they might recognize their mutual guilt, and so that when He, the Merciful Judge, should come to them, they might beseech Him. The coming of the serpent was not delayed, lest the beautiful sight of the tree should increase the temptation. But the Judge delayed His coming, to give the first parents an opportunity to prepare themselves for entreaty. But the haste of the tempter did not help them, although that haste could have served their benefit; nor did they make use of the delay of the Judge, although that delay had the same aim.

“And they heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and they hid themselves… from the presence of the Lord God amongst the trees of the garden” (Gen 3:8). It was not by His long-suffering alone, shown to the first parents, that God wished to help them, but also by the sound of His footsteps He desired to come to their aid; His soft footsteps gave forth a sound for the very purpose that they might prepare themselves to entreat the One who sent that sound. But when neither His delay nor the sound that went before Him brought them to come and stand before Him in silence, then God added to the sound of His footsteps the voice of His own mouth and said: “Adam, where art thou?” (Gen 3:9). But Adam, instead of confessing his guilt and entreating the Merciful One before He should pronounce upon him the sentence of judgment, says: “I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I saw that I was naked; and I hid myself” (Gen 3:10). The sound of footsteps that preceded God and announced Adam’s condemnation prefigured that voice of John which must go before the Son of God: “Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor… but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable… the wheat… he will gather into his garner” (Matt 3:12).

“I heard thy voice and I was afraid.” But when had you heard His voice, that you should hear it now? Behold, when He created you, brought you into paradise, brought sleep upon you, took your rib, made and brought a wife to you, you did not hear His voice. If, then, to hear His voice is something altogether new to you, understand at least now that the sound of the divine footsteps was for this purpose, that your lips might offer a prayer. So tell God, before He questions you, of the coming of the serpent, of your transgression and Eve’s. The confession of your lips may perhaps cleanse you from the sin which your hands committed when they plucked the fruit. But the first parents did not confess what they themselves had done, but told the All-Knowing One of what was going on within them.

“Adam, where art thou?” Hast thou become a god, as the serpent promised thee, or hast thou fallen under the power of death, as I threatened thee, if thou shouldest eat of the fruit of the tree? Consider, Adam: if instead of the serpent that came to thee, this most contemptible creature, an angel or some other higher being had come to thee, would it have been right for thee to despise the commandment of Him who gave thee everything, and to listen to the promise of one who had not till then done thee any good? Would it have been right to think Him not good who created thee out of nothing, made thee a second god over the universe, but to think him good who only in words promised thee good? Even if in a manifestation of power some higher being had come to thee and offered his promises, even then thou shouldst not have acted thus. The less should it have been so when the serpent came to thee, showing neither signs nor wonders. But thou, on the basis of one empty word he spoke, didst lie to thy God and believed a liar; thou didst account false Him who had given thee all good things and made thee lord of all; thou didst account true the liar, who dealt with thee maliciously to deprive thee of all dominion.

If the serpent had been forbidden to come and tempt Adam, those who now complain that the serpent came would then have complained that the serpent was forbidden to come. They would have asserted that the serpent was forbidden to come to Adam out of envy, lest after a brief temptation Adam should acquire eternal life. And those who now say that if the serpent had not come, Adam would not have sinned, would have asserted that if the serpent had come, Adam would not have sinned. As they are now convinced of the rightness of what they assert (that if the serpent had not come, Adam and Eve would not have sinned), so they would have been still more convinced of the rightness of saying that if the serpent (after God’s forbidding) had come, they would not have sinned. And which of these would be the more probable, if the event itself did not show that Adam obeyed the serpent and Eve submitted to a creeping thing?

“I heard thy voice and was afraid… and I hid myself,” because he kept silent about what he ought to have said, and instead spoke of what he need not have said. Instead of confessing what he himself had done, which would have been far more useful for him, Adam recounts what was happening within him — that is, he speaks of what was useless to him. God says to him: “Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee… that thou shouldest not eat?” (Gen 3:11). Thou hast seen thy nakedness by the sight which was given thee by the tree which promised thee divine sight. But Adam does not confess his guilt, and accuses his fellow-being, his wife: “The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat” (Gen 3:12) — it was not I who drew near to the tree, but my hand was stretched out to the forbidden fruit. So also the apostle says that Adam did not sin, but Eve transgressed the commandment (1 Tim 2:14). But if God gave thee, Adam, a wife, He gave her so that she might help thee and not harm thee, that she might be in subjection to thee and not command thee.

When Adam refused to confess his guilt, God turns the question to Eve and says: “What is this that thou hast done?” (Gen 3:13). And Eve, instead of entreating with tears and taking the guilt upon herself, as though unwilling to intercede for forgiveness for herself and her husband, does not say what promise the serpent had made her and by what he had persuaded her, but says simply: “The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat” (Gen 3:13). When both had been questioned, and it became evident that they had neither repentance nor true justification, then God turns to the serpent, not with a question, but with a sentence of punishment. For where there was room for repentance, a question was put; but for the one who is a stranger to repentance, the judgment is pronounced at once. That the serpent could not bring forth repentance we understand from the words of God: “Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle” (Gen 3:14); whereupon the serpent does not say that he did not do this (the temptation), for he fears to lie, but neither does he say that he did, because repentance is foreign to him.

“Cursed art thou above all cattle,” because thou hast deceived those set as lords over all cattle. And since thou art craftier than all the beasts, thou shalt be cursed “above every beast of the field… and upon thy belly shalt thou go” (Gen 3:14); for thou hast subjected the woman to the pains of childbearing. “Dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life” — because thou hast deprived Adam and Eve of the fruit of the tree of life. “And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her Seed” (Gen 3:15) — because thou hast deceived the woman and the offspring she brings forth, and enslaved them to thy death by thy treacherous love. Explaining the enmity which is placed between the serpent and the woman, between the seed of the serpent and the Seed of the woman, God says: “He shall crush thy head” (Gen 3:15) — that head which thou wast wishing to free from the bondage of her Seed; and thou shalt strike him (the serpent) not in the ear, but in the heel.

Although in strict justice the sentence of judgment ought to have been pronounced first to the serpent — for where the beginning of the transgression is, there ought punishment also to begin — God began with this contemptible one, of course in order that, while the wrath of justice was directed against him alone, Adam and Eve might come to fear and repent, and so the way might be opened for His goodness to deliver them from the curses of justice. But when the serpent had been cursed, and Adam and Eve did not resort to entreaties, God pronounces punishment upon them. He turns first to Eve, because by her hand sin was given to Adam, and pronounces the sentence upon her, saying: “I will greatly multiply thy sorrows and thy conception, and in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children” (Gen 3:16). Although Eve would have brought forth by the blessing of childbearing given her with all living creatures, yet she would have brought forth few, because those born of her would have been immortal. Moreover, she would have been free from the pains of childbearing, from the cares of raising the children born, and from sorrows for their death. “And thy desire shall be to thy husband,” that thou shouldest be under his rule, and not thyself rule, “and he shall rule over thee” (Gen 3:16), because thou hast hoped, after eating the fruits of the tree, to rule over him thyself.

When God had pronounced sentence on Eve, no repentance appeared in Adam; then He lays punishment on him also and says: “Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast consented to eat of the tree of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it… cursed is the ground for thy sake” (Gen 3:17). Although the innocent earth is punished for guilty Adam, yet by the curse on the earth, which cannot suffer, Adam, who could suffer, was made to suffer. Since the earth was cursed, he too came under the curse, who was not cursed. God did not say that the curse upon the earth would turn into punishment for man — for to man himself He also pronounced sentence, saying: “In sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life” (Gen 3:17), because of the transgression of the commandment. Just as in keeping the commandment thou wouldst have eaten of its fruits without sorrow, so now, after sin, the earth “shall bring forth thorns and thistles unto thee” — which it would not have produced, hadst thou not sinned. “Thou shalt eat the herb of the field,” for, attending to the empty seduction of thy wife, thou hast despised the desirable fruits of paradise; “in the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat thy bread,” because it did not please thee, without any toil, to enjoy the delights of the garden of Eden. And so it shall continue for thee, “till thou return unto the ground out of which thou wast taken” — for thou hast despised the commandment which would soon have given thee eternal life, while it was not yet permitted thee to eat of the fruits of the tree of life. And since thou art from the earth, but hast forgotten this, “thou shalt return unto the ground” (Gen 3:18–19) — and through this humiliation thou shalt know what thou art.

And Satan, created in those same six days when also was created the serpent, into which he had entered, was up to that sixth day as beautiful as Adam and Eve were beautiful before the transgression of the commandment. But Satan, who on that same day had become secretly Satan, was then also secretly accused and condemned; for God did not will to reveal to the first parents His judgment over him, lest they should be able to foresee his temptations. Therefore the woman said: “the serpent beguiled me” (Gen 3:13) — and not “Satan.”

So Satan was condemned in secret, and with him all his hosts were condemned. The sin is great, but the punishment would seem small if it had befallen only one or a few. But now, just as to Eve and all her daughters were appointed the pains of childbearing, as to Adam and all his children were appointed sorrows and death, as to the serpent and all its seed it was appointed to be trampled — so also to the one who was in the serpent, with all his hosts, it was appointed to go into the fire. But this is hidden in the Old Testament and revealed in the New Testament by our Lord, who says: “Of judgment, because the prince of this world hath been judged” (John 16:11) — that is, he (Satan) was at that time accused.

Having spoken of the punishment which the tempter and the tempted bore, Moses writes how “unto Adam also and to his wife the Lord God made coats of skins, and clothed them” (Gen 3:21). Those coats were either made from the skins of animals, or created anew, because, according to Moses, the Lord made these coats and clothed Adam and Eve with them. We may suppose that the first parents, touching with their hands the girdles around them, found that they were clothed in coats of skins of animals slain, perhaps before their very eyes, that they might feed on their flesh, cover their nakedness with skins, and in the very death of the animals see the death of their own bodies. After this “God said: Behold, the man is become as one of Us, to know good and evil” (Gen 3:22). In the words, “is become as one of Us,” God reveals the mystery of the Holy Trinity; but at the same time He mocks Adam, recalling what the serpent had said: “Ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil.”

Of course, Adam and Eve after eating the fruit of the tree knew good and evil, but even before eating they knew good by experience; of evil they had only heard. After eating, the opposite happened: of good they could now only hear, and of evil they had practical experience, for God took from them the glory in which they were clothed, and sorrows took possession of them which had not touched them before. “And now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever” (Gen 3:22). If Adam dared to eat of the fruit of the tree from which it was forbidden to eat, how much more would he rush to the tree of which it was not forbidden to eat? But since God had already appointed that the first parents should pass their life in toil and sweat, in sorrows and pains, lest, eating the fruit of the tree of life and receiving eternal life, they should be tortured eternally in this life, God did not allow those who had fallen under the curse to taste the fruit of that tree which He was preparing (for their immortality), to give it to them when they should be free from curses and clothed with glory. He did so that the life-giving gift might not become to them a cause of misery, and that what they would receive from the tree of life might not bring them greater misfortune compared to what the tree of knowledge had brought them. From the one they received temporal sicknesses, while the other would have made temporal sicknesses eternal; from the one they acquired death, which releases them from the bonds of sicknesses, while the other would have made them buried alive, because it would have preserved their life for eternal torment in sicknesses. Therefore God took away from them the tree of life. Moreover, it was unfitting to have a blessed life on the earth of curses, and to find eternal life in a world that passes away.

If the first parents had eaten of the fruit of the tree of life, one of two things would have happened: either the death-sentence of the judgment would have remained unfulfilled, or the tree of life would no longer have been life-giving. Therefore, so that the death-sentence of the judgment should not be broken and the tree of life should not prove ineffective and not life-giving, God expelled Adam from paradise, so that he should not suffer harm from the tree of life too, as he had suffered from the tree of knowledge. And He sent him forth “to till the ground from whence he was taken” (Gen 3:23), so that, working the earth, he might gain benefit, he to whom the rest of paradise had brought harm. After the first parents’ expulsion from paradise, God, as is written, on the east side of “the garden of Eden… set Cherubim, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life” (Gen 3:24). The bulwark of paradise had life, for it could turn of itself to guard the way to the tree of life from anyone who might desire its fruit and dare to pluck it. But the edge of the sword would have struck down the mortal who came to seize immortal life.
